text: Nexus is a contextual multi-armed bandit.
The objective is easy to state and hard to do well:
for every user, on every channel, choose the message that maximizes a scalar reward derived from engagement.
caption: maximize a scalar reward from engagement

text: To choose well, Nexus first has to represent the user.
Every user becomes a point in a ten-dimensional feature space.
caption: Each user → a point in 10-D feature space

text: The first six dimensions are behavioral, pulled from Nexus's own decision and conversion logs:
caption: Behavioral [0–5] — from decision & conversion logs

text: push and email conversion rates, three temporal ratios for morning, evening, and weekend, and an overall conversion rate.
caption: channel rates · morning/evening/weekend · overall rate

text: The last four are semantic, synced from YouVersion through Hightouch:
caption: Semantic [6–9] — synced via Hightouch

text: a recency score that decays over ninety days, a giving tier, a spiritual-depth composite of five signals, and a log-scaled engagement frequency.
caption: recency · giving tier · spiritual depth · frequency

text: Every dimension is normalized to roughly zero to one, so no single signal can dominate.
caption: all normalized to ~[0, 1]

text: Why normalize? Because Nexus compares users by cosine distance, not Euclidean distance.
Cosine measures the angle between two vectors, so it's invariant to magnitude.
caption: cosine = angle, invariant to magnitude

text: A user with a thousand sends and one with ten, at the same rate, point the same direction.
Those vectors are clustered into personas with k-means plus plus over cosine distance.
caption: k-means++ over cosine distance

text: Seed centroids far apart, then alternate an assignment step and an update step until no user changes cluster.
caption: assign → update → repeat until stable

text: It doesn't fix the number of personas. It runs every k, scores each with the mean silhouette, and keeps the cleanest separation.
caption: silhouette score picks the best k

text: as long as it clears a minimum of zero point two five. Five stability runs guard against an unlucky start.
caption: min silhouette 0.25 · 5 stability runs

text: Each persona then gets its own independent bandit.
Now the decision. Each message variant is an arm, and the default algorithm is Thompson Sampling.
Every arm carries a Beta distribution: alpha, the accumulated success, and beta, the accumulated failure.
caption: each arm = Beta(α, β)

text: The expected rate is alpha over alpha plus beta, and the variance shrinks as evidence accumulates.
caption: E[θ] = α / (α + β)

text: At send time, draw one sample from every arm and send whichever draws highest.
caption: draw θ ~ Beta(α,β) · send argmax

text: A confident arm draws high most of the time, but an uncertain, wide arm occasionally draws higher and earns an exploratory send.
caption: explore & exploit in one rule

text: New arms don't start neutral. They're initialized at Beta one, thirty — an expected value near three percent.
caption: pessimistic prior Beta(1, 30) ≈ 3%

text: A uniform prior would flood unproven variants with traffic. It takes about thirty sends to compete on merit.
When a decision resolves, engagement becomes reward. Goals are tiered — a plan completion is worth far more than an open.
The raw reward is divided by a hundred and clamped, so even a top conversion nudges alpha by about a tenth.
caption: r = clamp(raw / 100, −1, 1)

text: The update is asymmetric: a success adds the fractional reward to alpha, a failure adds exactly one to beta.
caption: r>0: α += r   else: β += 1

text: Attribution links each send to its outcome: forty-eight hours for standard goals, thirty days for long-horizon ones,
caption: 48h standard · 30d long-horizon

text: and a ninety-day retroactive penalty when a user disables push — the most aggressive signal in the system.
caption: push disabled → 90-day penalty

text: And after every update, alpha and beta both decay by zero point nine nine — a half-life of about sixty-nine updates.
caption: α, β ×= 0.99 · half-life ≈ 69

text: Put it together and you have a loop: represent, cluster, sample, send, observe, update, decay — every hour.
caption: represent · cluster · sample · send · observe · update · decay

text: Against a uniform-random baseline, Nexus delivers a thirty-four percent lift in production,
caption: +34% lift over random

text: and a two-proportion z-test, with confidence intervals, tells you when that lift is real. That's the core of Nexus.
caption: z-test + CIs confirm significance
